UI)\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [omnivoice/cli/demo.py](omnivoice/cli/demo.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThe `omnivoice-demo` CLI provides a graphical interface for interacting with the OmniVoice model. It allows users to perform zero-shot voice cloning, voice design, and automated text-to-speech without writing code. The UI is built using the [Gradio](https://gradio.app/) library and exposes the core capabilities of the `OmniVoice.generate()` pipeline.\n\n## CLI Usage and Configuration\n\nThe demo is launched via the `omnivoice-demo` entry point. It supports several flags to configure the server and model backend.\n\n| Option | Default | Description |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `--model` | `k2-fsa/OmniVoice` | Model checkpoint path or HuggingFace repo ID [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:120-123]](). |\n| `--device` | `None` | Device to use (e.g., `cuda`, `mps`, `cpu`). Auto-detected if not specified [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:125-126]](). |\n| `--ip` | `0.0.0.0` | Server IP address [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:127]](). |\n| `--port` | `7860` | Server port [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:129-130]](). |\n| `--root-path` | `None` | Root path for reverse proxy setups [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:132-135]](). |\n| `--share` | `False` | Create a public Gradio share link [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:137-138]](). |\n| `--no-asr` | `False` | Skip loading Whisper ASR for reference audio transcription [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:140-145]](). |\n| `--asr-model` | `openai/whisper-large-v3-turbo` | Whisper model version to use for auto-transcription [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:147-151]](). |\n\n**Device Auto-detection Logic:**\nThe script uses `get_best_device()` to prioritize hardware acceleration in the following order: `cuda` -\u003e `mps` -\u003e `cpu` [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:38-44]]().\n\n**Sources:**\n- [omnivoice/cli/demo.py:38-44]()\n- [omnivoice/cli/demo.py:113-152]()\n\n---\n\n## UI Layout and Functionality\n\nThe interface is organized into two primary tabs: **Voice Cloning** and **Voice Design**.\n\n### Voice Cloning Tab\nThis tab implements the `VoiceClonePrompt` workflow [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:206-212]]().\n- **Reference Audio**: An upload component for the source voice [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:236]]().\n- **Reference Text (Optional)**: If provided, this text is used as the transcript for the reference audio. If left blank, the system invokes Whisper ASR (unless `--no-asr` is set) to transcribe the audio automatically [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:209-212]]().\n- **Target Text**: The text to be synthesized in the cloned voice.\n\n### Voice Design Tab\nThis tab allows users to \"construct\" a voice using categorical attributes.\n- **Attribute Selectors**: Dropdowns for Gender, Age, Pitch, Style, English Accent, and Chinese Dialect [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:58-101]]().\n- **Instruction Assembly**: Selecting options from these categories dynamically builds a comma-separated `instruct` string (e.g., \"Female, Young Adult, High Pitch\") which is passed to the model [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:214-215]]().\n\n### Shared Components\n- **Language Selector**: A dropdown containing over 600 supported languages derived from `LANG_NAMES` [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:50]](). Selecting \"Auto\" allows the model to infer the language from the input text.\n- **Generation Parameters**: Sliders for `num_step` (diffusion steps), `guidance_scale`, `denoise`, `speed`, and `duration` [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:187-204]]().\n\n**Sources:**\n- [omnivoice/cli/demo.py:50]()\n- [omnivoice/cli/demo.py:58-101]()\n- [omnivoice/cli/demo.py:187-215]()\n- [omnivoice/cli/demo.py:236]()\n\n---\n\n## Data Flow: UI to Model\n\nThe Gradio UI acts as a wrapper around `OmniVoice.generate()`. When the \"Generate\" button is clicked, the `_gen_core` function is triggered.\n\n### Internal Logic Diagram (UI to Model Mapping)\n\nThis diagram illustrates how UI components map to the `OmniVoice` class methods and the `OmniVoiceGenerationConfig` entity.\n\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Gradio UI (omnivoice/cli/demo.py)\"\n        UI_Text[\"Text Input\"]\n        UI_Lang[\"Language Dropdown\"]\n        UI_RefAudio[\"Audio Upload (Cloning)\"]\n        UI_Instruct[\"Attribute Selectors (Design)\"]\n        UI_Params[\"Step/Scale/Speed/Denoise Sliders\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Logic Layer (_gen_core)\"\n        Config[\"OmniVoiceGenerationConfig\"]\n        PromptBuilder[\"model.create_voice_clone_prompt()\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Core Model (omnivoice/model.py)\"\n        Gen[\"OmniVoice.generate()\"]\n    end\n\n    UI_Params --\u003e|\"Maps to fields\"| Config\n    UI_RefAudio --\u003e|\"Passed to\"| PromptBuilder\n    UI_Text --\u003e|\"text=\"| Gen\n    UI_Lang --\u003e|\"language=\"| Gen\n    UI_Instruct --\u003e|\"instruct=\"| Gen\n    Config --\u003e|\"generation_config=\"| Gen\n    PromptBuilder --\u003e|\"voice_clone_prompt=\"| Gen\n```\n\n**Sources:**\n- [omnivoice/cli/demo.py:169-218]()\n- [omnivoice/model.py:125-206]()\n\n### Implementation Detail: `_gen_core`\n\nThe `_gen_core` function handles the conversion of UI state into model-ready tensors and configurations:\n1.  **Config Initialization**: It instantiates `OmniVoiceGenerationConfig` using values from the UI sliders, specifically setting `num_step`, `guidance_scale`, and `denoise` [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:187-193]]().\n2.  **Prompt Preparation**:\n    - For **Cloning**: It calls `model.create_voice_clone_prompt(ref_audio=ref_audio, ref_text=ref_text)` [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:209-212]]().\n    - For **Design**: It cleans the instruction string from the UI and adds it to the keyword arguments [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:214-215]]().\n3.  **Inference**: It executes `model.generate(**kw)` where `kw` contains the text, language, and the prepared prompts/configs [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:218]]().\n4.  **Post-processing**: The output `torch.Tensor` is converted to a 16-bit PCM NumPy array for Gradio's audio player [[omnivoice/cli/demo.py:221-224]]().\n\n**Sources:**\n- [omnivoice/cli/demo.py:187-224]()\n\n---\n\n## Interaction Sequence\n\nThe following diagram shows the sequence of events when a user generates as OmniVoice (model.py)\n    participant Whisper as Whisper ASR (Optional)\n\n    User-\u003e\u003eGradio: Upload ref_audio \u0026 Input text\n    User-\u003e\u003eGradio: Click 'Generate'\n create_voice_clone_prompt(ref_audio, ref_text)\n        Model--\u003e\u003eCore: VoiceClonePrompt Object\n    end\n\n    Core-\u003e\u003eModel: generate(text, voice_clone_prompt, generation_config)\n    Model--\u003e\u003eCore: torch.Tensor\n    Core-\u003e\u003eGradio: Convert to int16 NumPy array\n    Gradio--\u003e\u003eUser: Playable Audio\n```\n\n**Sources:**\n- files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/data_preparation.md](docs/data_preparation.md)\n- [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py](omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py)\n- [omnivoice/utils/audio.py](omnivoice/utils/audio.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThe OmniVoice data pipeline is designed to transform raw, heterogeneous audio collections into a unified, high-performance training format based on **WebDataset**. This architecture minimizes disk I/O bottlenecks by packing thousands of small audio files into sequential tar shards, paired with external JSONL metadata for flexible filtering and modification.\n\nThe pipeline transitions data from the **Natural Language Space** (raw transcripts and file paths) into the **Code Entity Space** (discrete multi-layer tokens) through a series of specialized scripts and processing stages.\n\n### Pipeline Flow Overview\n\nThe following diagram illustrates the standard progression from raw input to training-ready manifests, highlighting the primary scripts involved in the transformation.\n\n**Title: OmniVoice Data Transformation Flow**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Input Space\"\n        A[\"Raw Audio (.wav/.flac)\"] \n        B[\"JSONL Manifest\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Processing Stage (Scripts)\"\n        C[\"jsonl_to_webdataset.py\"]\n        D[\"extract_audio_tokens.py\"]\n        E[\"denoise_audio.py\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Output Space (WebDataset)\"\n        F[\"shard-000000.tar (Audio Tokens)\"]\n        G[\"shard-000000.jsonl (Metadata)\"]\n        H[\"data.lst (Global Manifest)\"]\n    end\n\n    B --\u003e C\n    A --\u003e C\n    C -- \"Raw Audio Shards\" --\u003e D\n    B -- \"Direct Input\" --\u003e D\n    D --\u003e F\n    D --\u003e G\n    D --\u003e H\n    \n    A -- \"Optional\" --\u003e E\n    E -- \"Denoised Shards\" --\u003e D\n```\nSources: [docs/data_preparation.md:3-42](), [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:18-49]()\n\n---\n\n### Standard Data Preparation\nThe standard pipeline focuses on converting raw audio and text transcripts into discrete audio tokens. OmniVoice utilizes the `higgs-audio-v2-tokenizer` to represent audio as an 8-layer matrix of discrete codes stored as `.npy` files within tar archives [docs/data_preparation.md:144-152]().\n\nKey components include:\n*   **`jsonl_to_webdataset.py`**: A utility to pack raw audio files into WebDataset shards. It handles resampling via `torchaudio.functional.resample` and uses `ProcessPoolExecutor` for parallel processing [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:154-194]().\n*   **`extract_audio_tokens.py`**: The primary entry point that performs tokenization and sharding. It can consume either raw JSONL manifests or existing raw-audio WebDatasets [docs/data_preparation.md:24-57]().\n*   **`data.lst`**: A manifest file where each line contains the absolute path to a `.tar` shard, its companion `.jsonl` metadata, the sample count, and the total duration [docs/data_preparation.md:124-133]().\n\nFor a step-by-step guide on running these scripts and the required JSONL schema, see [Standard Data Preparation](#4.1).\n\nSources: [docs/data_preparation.md:6-70](), [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:37-48](), [omnivoice/utils/audio.py:69-88]()\n\n---\n\n### Advanced Data Preparation\nFor datasets with environmental noise or varying recording quality, OmniVoice provides an advanced pipeline that integrates speech enhancement and robustness augmentation.\n\n*   **Denoising**: Uses the `Sidon` model via `denoise_audio.py` to extract clean speech from noisy backgrounds before tokenization.\n*   **Noise Augmentation**: The script `extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py` injects environmental noise and Room Impulse Responses (RIR) into the prompt prefix. This ensures the model remains robust to noisy reference audio during zero-shot voice cloning.\n\nFor details on Sidon model integration and RIR augmentation, see [Advanced Data Preparation (Denoising \u0026 Noise Augmentation)](#4.2).\n\nSources: [docs/data_preparation.md:182-182]()\n\n---\n\n### Dataset and DataLoader Internals\nThe training engine consumes the processed shards through a sophisticated loading stack designed for high-throughput multi-node training. The pipeline handles complex logic such as sequence packing to minimize padding and length-based bucketing.\n\n**Title: Data Loading Architecture (Code Entity Space)**\n```mermaid\ngraph BT\n    subgraph \"PyTorch DataLoader\"\n        Collator[\"PackingDataCollator\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Iterable Stack\"\n        Packer[\"PackingIterableDataset\"]\n        Bucket[\"StreamLengthGroupDataset\"]\n        Processor[\"OmniVoiceSampleProcessor\"]\n        Mux[\"MuxWebDatasetReader\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Source Shards\"\n        S1[\"WebDatasetReader (Dataset A)\"]\n        S2[\"WebDatasetReader (Dataset B)\"]\n    end\n\n    S1 --\u003e Mux\n    S2 --\u003e Mux\n    Mux --\u003e Processor\n    Processor --\u003e Bucket\n    Bucket --\u003e Packer\n    Packer --\u003e Collator\n```\n\nThe loading stack includes:\n*   **`WebDatasetReader`**: Reads `.npy` tokens and `.jsonl` metadata from shards.\n*   **`OmniVoiceSampleProcessor`**: Handles runtime logic including language/instruction token injection and the diffusion masking strategy.\n*   **`PackingIterableDataset`**: Efficiently packs multiple short samples into a single training sequence of fixed length [docs/data_preparation.md:3-4]().\n*   **Data Config**: A JSON structure that defines training/validation sets, manifest paths, and dataset weights via the `repeat` parameter [docs/data_preparation.md:158-178]().\n\nFor technical details on the `OmniVoiceSampleProcessor` and `PackingIterableDataset`, see [Dataset and DataLoader Internals](#4.3).\n\nSources: [docs/data_preparation.md:3-4](), [docs/data_preparation.md:158-178](), Preparation\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/data_preparation.md](docs/data_preparation.md)\n- [examples/config/data_config_emilia.json](examples/config/data_config_emilia.json)\n- [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py](omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py)\n- [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py](omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py)\n- [omnivoice/utils/audio.py](omnivoice/utils/audio.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThis page details the standard pipeline for transforming raw audio datasets into the optimized WebDataset format required for OmniVoice training. The pipeline ensures efficient disk I/O by packing samples into tar shards and provides high-performance audio tokenization using discrete codebooks.\n\n## Overview of the Pipeline\n\nThe data preparation process typically follows one of two paths:\n1.  **Direct Tokenization**: Converting a raw JSONL manifest directly into audio token shards.\n2.  **Two-Stage Preparation**: First packing raw audio into tar shards (FLAC), then tokenizing those shards into discrete audio tokens.\n\nThe final output is a set of `.tar` files containing `.npy` token arrays and corresponding `.jsonl` metadata files, indexed by a `data.lst` manifest [docs/data_preparation.md:96-121]().\n\n### Data Flow Diagram\n\nThe following diagram illustrates the transformation from raw files to training-ready shards.\n\n\"Data Preparation Flow\"\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Input Space\"\n        A[\"Raw Audio (.wav/.flac/.mp3)\"]\n        B[\"JSONL Manifest\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"jsonl_to_webdataset.py\"\n        C[\"ProcessPoolExecutor\"]\n        D[\"load_waveform \u0026 resample\"]\n        E[\"WebDataset TarWriter\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"extract_audio_tokens.py\"\n        F[\"HiggsAudioV2TokenizerModel\"]\n        G[\"Discrete Tokens (8 layers)\"]\n        H[\"Sharded WebDataset (.tar + .jsonl)\"]\n    end\n\n    B --\u003e C\n    A --\u003e D\n    C --\u003e D\n    D --\u003e E\n    E -- \"Raw Audio Shards (FLAC)\" --\u003e F\n    B -- \"Direct Path\" --\u003e F\n    F --\u003e G\n    G --\u003e H\n    H --\u003e I[\"data.lst Manifest\"]\n```\n**Sources:** [docs/data_preparation.md:24-42](), [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:19-49](), [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:19-45](), [omnivoice/utils/audio.py:45-67]()\n\n---\n\n## 1. Raw Audio Sharding (`jsonl_to_webdataset.py`)\n\nThis script is used to pack raw audio files into WebDataset shards. It resamples audio to a target sample rate (default 24kHz) and saves them as FLAC files within tar archives to save space while maintaining quality [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:23-29]().\n\n### Key Components\n- **`load_waveform`**: Located in `omnivoice.utils.audio`, it attempts to load audio using `soundfile` and falls back to `librosa` for formats like MP3/M4A [omnivoice/utils/audio.py:45-67]().\n- **`process_audio_item`**: Loads audio, calculates `audio_duration`, and resamples using `torchaudio.functional.resample` if the source rate differs from the target [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:154-190]().\n- **`ProcessPoolExecutor`**: Distributes shard creation across multiple CPU cores [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:58-64]().\n- **`wds.TarWriter`**: Writes the processed FLAC bytes into the tar archive using the `__key__` (from the sample ID) and `flac` extension [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:185-188]().\n\n### Output Format\nThe script produces two parallel directories:\n- `audios/`: Contains `.tar` files with audio samples [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:39-42]().\n- `txts/`: Contains `.jsonl` files with metadata (including calculated `audio_duration`) [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:43-46]().\n\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/scripts/jsonl_to_webdataset.py:154-243](), [omnivoice/utils/audio.py:45-89](), [docs/data_preparation.md:59-68]()\n\n---\n\n## 2. Audio Tokenization (`extract_audio_tokens.py`)\n\nThis is the primary script for generating training data. It uses the `HiggsAudioV2TokenizerModel` to convert audio into 8 layers of discrete tokens [docs/data_preparation.md:24-25]().\n\n### Implementation Details\nThe script utilizes a multi-GPU processing strategy. Each worker process is initialized via `process_init`, which assigns a specific GPU and loads the tokenizer and feature extractor onto that device [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:187-217]().\n\n\"Tokenization Code Entity Map\"\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"omnivoice.scripts.extract_audio_tokens\"\n        START[\"main()\"]\n        POOL[\"ProcessPoolExecutor\"]\n        INIT[\"process_init()\"]\n        SAMP[\"process_single_sample()\"]\n        SER[\"serialise_numpy()\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Model Entities (HuggingFace)\"\n        TOK[\"HiggsAudioV2TokenizerModel\"]\n        EXT[\"AutoFeatureExtractor\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Data Readers (omnivoice.data.dataset)\"\n        JSR[\"JsonlDatasetReader\"]\n        WDR[\"WebDatasetReader\"]\n    end\n\n    START --\u003e POOL\n    POOL --\u003e INIT\n    INIT --\u003e TOK\n    INIT --\u003e EXT\n    POOL --\u003e SAMP\n    SAMP --\u003e JSR\n    SAMP --\u003e WDR\n    SAMP --\u003e TOK\n    SAMP --\u003e SER\n```\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:187-217](), [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:65-66](), [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:181-184]()\n\n### Sharding Parameters\n| Parameter | Default | Function |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `--samples_per_shard` | 1000 | Max records per tar file [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:103-107](). |\n| `--min_num_shards` | 32 | Ensures enough shards for distributed data loading [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:109-114](). |\n| `--nj_per_gpu` | 3 | Number of worker processes to spawn per GPU [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:150-154](). |\n| `--min_length` / `--max_length` | 0.0 / inf | Filters audio by duration (seconds) [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:125-135](). |\n\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:103-172](), [docs/data_preparation.md:76-94]()\n\n---\n\n## 3. Manifest and Configuration\n\n### The `data.lst` Format\nThe manifest file is the single point of entry for the trainer to locate data. Each line describes one shard [docs/data_preparation.md:126-133]():\n` \u003ctar_path\u003e \u003cjsonl_path\u003e \u003cnum_samples\u003e \u003ctotal_duration_seconds\u003e `\n\n- **Tar Shard**: Contains `.npy` files created via `serialise_numpy`. Each file is a numpy array of shape `[8, T]` and dtype `int16` [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens.py:181-184](), [docs/data_preparation.md:147-152]().\n- **JSONL Shard**: Contains the original metadata for those specific samples, allowing metadata access without extracting the tar [docs/data_preparation.md:137]().\n\n### Training Data Config Schema\nThe training configuration references these manifests in a JSON structure, typically found in `examples/config/` [examples/config/data_config_emilia.json:1-36]().\n\n```json\n{\n    \"train\": [\n        {\n            \"language_id\": \"en\",\n            \"manifest_path\": [\"data/custom/tokens/train/data.lst\"],\n            \"repeat\": 1\n        }\n    ]\n}\n```\n- **`manifest_path`**: A list of absolute paths to `data.lst` files [docs/data_preparation.md:176]().\n- **`repeat`**: Integer used for dataset balancing during multi-task or multilingual training [docs/data_preparation.md:177]().\n- **`language_id`**: Used for organizational purposes in the config [docs/data_preparation.md:178]().\n\n**Sources:** [docs/data_preparation.md:124-180](), [examples/config/data_config_emilia.json:1-36]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"25:T1acd,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# Advanced Data Preparation (Denoising \u0026 Noise Augmentation)\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/data_preparation_advanced.md](docs/data_preparation_advanced.md)\n- [omnivoice/scripts/denoise_audio.py](omnivoice/scripts/denoise_audio.py)\n- [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py](omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThe advanced data preparation pipeline extends the standard workflow by adding speech enhancement and robust noise augmentation. These stages are designed to improve the quality of training data and make the model resilient to noisy reference prompts during inference [docs/data_preparation_advanced.md:1-4]().\n\n## Pipeline Overview\n\nThe advanced pipeline typically consists of two primary stages that chain together:\n\n1.  **Denoising**: Raw audio is processed through the Sidon speech enhancement model to remove background noise [docs/data_preparation_advanced.md:13-14]().\n2.  **Tokenization with Noise Augmentation**: Cleaned audio is tokenized, but a specific proportion of the **prompt prefix** (reference audio) is augmented with environmental noise and room impulse responses (RIR) [docs/data_preparation_advanced.md:16-17](), [docs/data_preparation_advanced.md:47-49]().\n\n### Data Flow and Code Entities\n\nThe following diagram illustrates how the scripts and models interact to transform raw audio into augmented tokens.\n\n**Advanced Preparation Data Flow**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Denoising Stage (denoise_audio.py)\"\n        A[\"Raw JSONL / data.lst\"] --\u003e B[\"JsonlDatasetReader / WebDatasetReader\"]\n        B --\u003e C[\"StreamLengthGroupDataset (Dynamic Batching)\"]\n        C --\u003e D[\"Sidon Model (Feature Extractor + Decoder)\"]\n        D --\u003e E[\"WebDataset Shards (.flac + .json)\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Tokenization Stage (extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py)\"\n        E --\u003e F[\"WebDatasetReader\"]\n        G[\"Noise Manifest (data.lst)\"] --\u003e H[\"SimpleWorkerSampler (Noise)\"]\n        I[\"RIR Manifest (data.lst)\"] --\u003e J[\"SimpleWorkerSampler (RIR)\"]\n        F \u0026 H \u0026 J --\u003e K[\"ProcessPoolExecutor Workers\"]\n        K --\u003e L[\"HiggsAudioV2TokenizerModel\"]\n        L --\u003e M[\"Augmented Token Shards (.npy + .json)\"]\n    end\n```\nSources: [omnivoice/scripts/denoise_audio.py:18-48](), [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py:18-51](), [docs/data_preparation_advanced.md:11-18]()\n\n---\n\n## Denoising with Sidon\n\nThe `denoise_audio.py` script utilizes the **Sidon** speech enhancement model to clean raw datasets. It supports high-throughput batch denoising by grouping audio by length to minimize padding overhead [omnivoice/scripts/denoise_audio.py:74]().\n\n### Implementation Details\n- **Model Architecture**: Sidon consists of a feature extractor and a decoder [omnivoice/scripts/denoise_audio.py:117-126]().\n- **Sample Rates**: Input is resampled to 16 kHz for the model and output at a target rate (default 24 kHz) [omnivoice/scripts/denoise_audio.py:78-79](), [omnivoice/scripts/denoise_audio.py:128-132]().\n- **Batching Strategy**: Uses `StreamLengthGroupDataset` for dynamic batching based on `batch_duration` [omnivoice/scripts/denoise_audio.py:150-154]().\n\n### Usage Example\n```bash\npython -m omnivoice.scripts.denoise_audio \\\n    --input_jsonl data.jsonl \\\n    --tar_output_pattern data/denoised/audios/shard-%06d.tar \\\n    --feature_extractor_path sidon_feature_extractor_cuda.pt \\\n    --decoder_path sidon_decoder_cuda.pt \\\n    --batch_duration 200.0\n```\nSources: [omnivoice/scripts/denoise_audio.py:30-35](), [docs/data_preparation_advanced.md:25-35]()\n\n---\n\n## Tokenization with Noise Augmentation\n\nThe `extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py` script extends the standard tokenization by injecting noise and reverberation into the reference portion of the audio [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py:21-23](). This ensures the model can handle non-studio quality reference clips during zero-shot voice cloning.\n\n### Augmentation Logic\nThe script samples noise and RIRs using `SimpleWorkerSampler`, which provides an infinite stream of augmentation data within each worker process [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py:219-222]().\n\n| Feature | Description | Code Entity |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| **Noise Sampling** | Environmental noise mixed at varying SNR | `SimpleWorkerSampler` |\n| **RIR Convolution** | Room impulse responses applied via FFT convolution | `SimpleWorkerSampler` |\n| **Prompt Focus** | Augmentation is applied primarily to the reference prefix | `extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py` |\n| **Tokenizer** | Discrete token extraction via Higgs | `HiggsAudioV2TokenizerModel` |\n\n### Implementation Mapping\nThe following diagram maps the augmentation logic to specific code entities.\n\n**Augmentation Logic Mapping**\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Worker Initialization\"\n        W[\"ProcessPoolExecutor\"] --\u003e I[\"init_worker()\"]\n        I --\u003e T[\"worker_tokenizer (Higgs)\"]\n        I --\u003e NS[\"worker_noise_sampler\"]\n        I --\u003e RS[\"worker_rir_sampler\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Processing Loop\"\n        D[\"Audio Data\"] --\u003e P[\"process_sample()\"]\n        NS --\u003e P\n        RS --\u003e P\n        P --\u003e T\n        T --\u003e O[\"Discrete Tokens (.npy)\"]\n    end\n```\nSources: [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py:84-89](), [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py:219-225](), [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py:73]()\n\n### Usage Example\n```bash\npython -m omnivoice.scripts.extract_audio_tokens_add_noise \\\n    --input_manifest data/denoised/data.lst \\\n    --noise_manifest data/noise_shards/data.lst \\\n    --rir_manifest data/rir_shards/data.lst \\\n    --tokenizer_path eustlb/higgs-audio-v2-tokenizer \\\n    --tar_output_pattern data/tokens/shard-%06d.tar\n```\nSources: [omnivoice/scripts/extract_audio_tokens_add_noise.py:28-32](), [docs/data_preparation_advanced.md:55-65]()\n\n## Key Classes and Functions\n\n| Entity | Location | Purpose |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `JsonlDatasetReader` | [omnivoice/data/dataset.py:75]() | Internals\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [omnivoice/data/__init__.py](omnivoice/data/__init__.py)\n- [omnivoice/data/batching.py](omnivoice/data/batching.py)\n- [omnivoice/data/collator.py](omnivoice/data/collator.py)\n- [omnivoice/data/dataset.py](omnivoice/data/dataset.py)\n- [omnivoice/data/processor.py](omnivoice/data/processor.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThis page details the training data layer of OmniVoice, covering the transition from raw WebDataset shards to packed, masked, and tokenized tensors ready for the diffusion language model. The pipeline handles multi-codebook audio tokens, text prompt injection, and efficient sequence packing to maximize GPU utilization.\n\n## Data Loading and Manifest Management\n\nOmniVoice uses a hierarchical manifest system to manage large-scale multilingual datasets. The primary entry point is a JSON configuration file that organizes data into `train` and `dev` splits [omnivoice/data/dataset.py:66-107]().\n\n### Manifest Structure\nA `data.lst` file contains space-separated entries for each shard:\n1.  **Tar Path**: Path to the WebDataset `.tar` file containing audio or token data.\n2.  **Label Path**: Path to the `.jsonl` file containing metadata (text, language, etc.).\n3.  **Num Items**: Number of samples in the shard.\n4.  **Num Seconds**: Total duration of audio in the shard.\n\nThe `prepare_data_manifests_from_json` function parses these manifests, supporting dataset repetition via the `repeat` field [omnivoice/data/dataset.py:143-149]().\n\n### Reader Classes\nThe codebase provides several reader implementations for different stages of the pipeline:\n\n| Class | Purpose | File Reference |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `WebDatasetReader` | Reads audio/text pairs from WebDataset tar shards as an iterable. | [omnivoice/data/dataset.py:29-32]() |\n| `MuxWebDatasetReader` | Multiplexes multiple readers to balance multilingual data. | [omnivoice/data/dataset.py:31-32]() |\n| `JsonlDatasetReader` | Reads from raw JSONL files (used primarily in preprocessing scripts). | [omnivoice/data/dataset.py:33-34]() |\n\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/data/dataset.py:29-190]()\n\n## Sample Processing and Token Injection\n\nThe `OmniVoiceSampleProcessor` is the core logic engine that transforms a raw sample (audio tokens + text) into a model-ready dictionary. It handles prompt creation, masking for the diffusion objective, and conditional dropout.\n\n### Processing Logic\n1.  **Conditioning Dropout**: Randomly drops text or prompts based on `drop_cond_ratio` to support unconditional generation [omnivoice/data/processor.py:75-82]().\n2.  **Style Injection**: Injects special tokens for language and instructions (e.g., `\u003c|lang_start|\u003een\u003c|lang_end|\u003e`) [omnivoice/data/processor.py:94-107]().\n3.  **Text Prompting**: Prepends the transcript (or Pinyin if `use_pinyin_ratio` is met) surrounded by `\u003c|text_start|\u003e` and `\u003c|text_end|\u003e` [omnivoice/data/processor.py:117-127]().\n4.  **Audio Masking**: \n    *   Determines a `prompt_length`. If `clean_start_token_idx` is present in the label (used for prompt denoising training), it uses that index; otherwise, it calculates it based on `prompt_ratio_range` [omnivoice/data/processor.py:133-136]().\n    *   Applies a random mask to the remaining \"maskable region\" using the `audio_mask_id` [omnivoice/data/processor.py:141-144]().\n    *   Sets labels to `-100` for unmasked tokens or prompt regions to exclude them from the loss calculation [omnivoice/data/processor.py:145-150]().\n\n### Data Flow: Natural Language to Code Entity\n\nThe following diagram illustrates how raw inputs are transformed into the `return_dict` by the `OmniVoiceSampleProcessor`.\n\n**Title: Sample Processor Transformation Flow**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Natural Language \u0026 Metadata\"\n        A[\"Text Transcript\"]\n        B[\"Language ID\"]\n        C[\"Instruct String\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"OmniVoiceSampleProcessor Entity Space\"\n        D[\"text_tokenizer\"]\n        E[\"style_inputs\"]\n        F[\"text_inputs\"]\n        G[\"audio_tokens\"]\n        H[\"audio_mask_id\"]\n    end\n\n    A --\u003e D\n    B --\u003e E\n    C --\u003e E\n    D --\u003e F\n    \n    subgraph \"Output Construction [omnivoice/data/processor.py]\"\n        I[\"torch.cat([style_inputs, text_inputs, audio_inputs])\"]\n        J[\"audio_mask [Boolean Tensor]\"]\n        K[\"labels [Loss Masking]\"]\n    end\n\n    E --\u003e I\n    F --\u003e I\n    G --\u003e I\n    H --\u003e I\n    I --\u003e L[\"return_dict\"]\n    J --\u003e L\n    K --\u003e L\n```\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/data/processor.py:35-172]()\n\n## Batching and Sequence Packing\n\nOmniVoice uses sequence packing instead of standard padding to maximize GPU efficiency. This is handled by `PackingIterableDataset` and `PackingDataCollator`.\n\n### Packing Mechanism\n*   **`PackingIterableDataset`**: Iterates through the processed samples and accumulates them until the total number of tokens reaches `batch_tokens` [omnivoice/data/batching.py:150-162]().\n*   **`PackingDataCollator`**: Concatenates multiple samples into a single continuous sequence. It generates `document_ids` to allow the model's attention mechanism (via Flash Attention) to distinguish between different samples within the same packed sequence [omnivoice/data/collator.py:79-90](). It also pads the final sequence to exactly `batch_tokens` using the tokenizer's `pad_token_id` [omnivoice/data/collator.py:56-60]().\n\n### Length Bucketing\nFor preprocessing tasks (like token extraction), the `StreamLengthGroupDataset` is used. It maintains multiple \"buckets\" based on audio duration to ensure that each batch contains samples of similar lengths, minimizing padding within the batch [omnivoice/data/batching.py:39-63]().\n\n**Title: Packing and Batching Entity Relationship**\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Dataset Layer\"\n        DR[\"IterableDataReader\"]\n        PI[\"PackingIterableDataset\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Processing Layer\"\n        OP[\"OmniVoiceSampleProcessor\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Collation Layer\"\n        PC[\"PackingDataCollator\"]\n    end\n\n    DR -- \"raw_sample\" --\u003e PI\n    PI -- \"calls\" --\u003e OP\n    OP -- \"processed_sample\" --\u003e PI\n    PI -- \"List[Dict]\" --\u003e PC\n    PC -- \"Dict[Tensors]\" --\u003e DL[\"DataLoader Output\"]\n\n    style PI stroke-width:2px\n    style PC stroke-width:2px\n```\n\n### Key Batching Classes\n\n| Class | Logic | Source |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `StreamLengthGroupDataset` | Groups by `boundaries` calculated via `np.linspace` [omnivoice/data/batching.py:63](). | [omnivoice/data/batching.py:39]() |\n| `PackingIterableDataset` | Aggregates until `current_token_count \u003e batch_tokens`. | [omnivoice/data/batching.py:108]() |\n| `PackingDataCollator` | Pads the final concatenated sequence to exactly `batch_tokens`. | [omnivoice/data/collator.py:30]() |\n\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/data/batching.py:1-167](), [omnivoice/data/collator.py:1-93]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"27:T17f2,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# Training\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/training.md](docs/training.md)\n- [omnivoice/training/builder.py](omnivoice/training/builder.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThe OmniVoice training system is designed for high-performance, large-scale training of diffusion language models. It leverages `HuggingFace Accelerate` and `DeepSpeed` to handle multi-GPU distribution, mixed-precision training, and efficient gradient accumulation. The system supports training from scratch on massive datasets like Emilia, as well as fine-tuning from pretrained checkpoints.\n\n### Training Overview\n\nTraining is driven by the `omnivoice.cli.train` entrypoint [docs/training.md:31-31](), which initializes the `OmniTrainer` to manage the optimization loop. The process is governed by two primary configuration files: a **Training Config** (hyperparameters, paths, logging) and a **Data Config** (dataset manifests and sampling ratios) [docs/training.md:5-5]().\n\nThe training setup is orchestrated by `omnivoice.training.builder.build_model_and_tokenizer`, which handles the expansion of the LLM vocabulary to include special tokens like `\u003c|lang_start|\u003e` and `\u003c|instruct_start|\u003e` [omnivoice/training/builder.py:49-79]().\n\n#### Core Components\n*   **Launcher**: Uses `accelerate launch` to manage distributed processes across multiple GPUs [docs/training.md:28-35]().\n*   **Optimizer**: Supports standard LLM optimization techniques, typically using AdamW with a peak learning rate around `1e-4` for scratch training [docs/training.md:20-20]().\n*   **Data Loading**: Utilizes a sophisticated pipeline involving `WebDataset` and `PackingIterableDataset` for streaming and packing large-scale tokenized audio data into fixed-size token batches [omnivoice/training/builder.py:125-150]().\n\n### Training Execution and Component Mapping\n\nThe following diagrams illustrate how the training command maps to the underlying code entities and how the system builds the model from a base LLM.\n\n**Training Execution Flow**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"CLI Layer\"\n        START[\"accelerate launch\"] --\u003e TRAIN_CLI[\"omnivoice.cli.train\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Configuration Space\"\n        TRAIN_CLI --\u003e T_CONFIG[\"TrainingConfig\"]\n        TRAIN_CLI --\u003e D_CONFIG[\"DataConfig\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Builder Logic\"\n        TRAIN_CLI --\u003e BUILDER[\"omnivoice.training.builder\"]\n        BUILDER --\u003e B_MOD[\"build_model_and_tokenizer()\"]\n        BUILDER --\u003e B_DATA[\"build_dataloaders()\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Logic Layer\"\n        TRAIN_CLI --\u003e OT[\"OmniTrainer Class\"]\n        OT --\u003e ACCEL[\"Accelerator (HF)\"]\n        OT --\u003e OPT[\"Optimizer \u0026 Scheduler\"]\n        OT --\u003e LOOP[\"Training Loop\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Persistence\"\n        LOOP --\u003e CKPT[\"save_checkpoint()\"]\n        LOOP --\u003e LOG[\"TrainLogger / TensorBoard\"]\n    end\n```\nSources: [docs/training.md:14-35](), [omnivoice/training/builder.py:18-27]()\n\n**Model Construction Mapping**\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Base Components\"\n        LLM[\"AutoModel (Qwen)\"]\n        TOKEN[\"AutoTokenizer\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"OmniVoice Assembly\"\n        B_MOD[\"build_model_and_tokenizer\"]\n        B_MOD --\u003e ADD_T[\"tokenizer.add_special_tokens\"]\n        B_MOD --\u003e RESIZE[\"model.llm.resize_token_embeddings\"]\n        B_MOD --\u003e OV[\"OmniVoice(config, llm)\"]\n    end\n\n    LLM --\u003e B_MOD\n    TOKEN --\u003e B_MOD\n```\nSources: [omnivoice/training/builder.py:49-122]()\n\n---\n\n### Launching and Resuming\n\n#### Starting Training\nTo start a new training run, specify the number of GPUs and the configuration files. The `batch_tokens` parameter in the config defines the number of tokens processed per GPU per step [docs/training.md:21-21]().\n\n```bash\naccelerate launch \\\n    --gpu_ids \"0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7\" \\\n    --num_processes 8 \\\n    -m omnivoice.cli.train \\\n    --train_config examples/config/train_config_emilia.json \\\n    --data_config examples/config/data_config_emilia.json \\\n    --output_dir exp/omnivoice_emilia\n```\nSources: [docs/training.md:27-35]()\n\n#### Resuming vs. Fine-tuning\nThe system distinguishes between resuming an interrupted run and starting a new fine-tuning task from a pretrained model.\n\n| Mode | Config Key | Behavior |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Resume** | `resume_from_checkpoint` | Restores model weights, optimizer state, and scheduler step [docs/training.md:39-45](). |\n| **Fine-tune** | `init_from_checkpoint` | Loads model weights via `OmniVoice.from_pretrained`; resets optimizer and scheduler for a new task [omnivoice/training/builder.py:80-87](), [docs/training.md:49-55](). |\n\nSources: [docs/training.md:37-55](), [omnivoice/training/builder.py:80-87]()\n\n---\n\n### Monitoring and Logging\n\nThe training system uses a `TrainLogger` to track metrics. By default, logs are written to TensorBoard within the specified `output_dir` [docs/training.md:59-62]().\n\n**Monitored Metrics:**\n*   **Loss**: Total diffusion loss and per-task losses.\n*   **Learning Rate**: Current LR after scheduler updates.\n*   **Throughput**: Tokens per second across the cluster.\n\n**Monitoring System Architecture**\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Execution\"\n        STEP[\"Training Step\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Logging System\"\n        STEP --\u003e TL[\"TrainLogger\"]\n        TL --\u003e TB[\"TensorBoard Event Files\"]\n        TL --\u003e STDOUT[\"Console Output\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Visualization\"\n        TB --\u003e VIEW[\"tensorboard --logdir\"]\n    end\n```\nSources: [docs/training.md:57-62]()\n\n---\n\n### Detailed Documentation\n\nFor specific details on configuring, implementing, or running training, refer to the following child pages:\n\n*   **[Training Configuration](#5.1)**: Complete reference for `TrainingConfig` fields, hyperparameters, and the JSON schema for data manifests.\n*   **[Training Loop and Checkpointing](#5.2)**: Deep dive into the `OmniTrainer` internals, including DeepSpeed integration, gradient accumulation, and the checkpoint saving mechanism.\n*   **[Training Examples and Scripts](#5.3)**: Practical guide for using `run_emilia.sh` and `run_finetune.sh` to automate the Configuration\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [examples/config/ds_config_zero2.json](examples/config/ds_config_zero2.json)\n- [examples/config/train_config_emilia.json](examples/config/train_config_emilia.json)\n- [examples/config/train_config_finetune.json](examples/config/train_config_finetune.json)\n- [examples/config/train_config_multilingual.json](examples/config/train_config_multilingual.json)\n- [omnivoice/training/config.py](omnivoice/training/config.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThis page provides a technical reference for the configuration systems used in OmniVoice training. It covers the `TrainingConfig` dataclass, the JSON schema for data manifests, and the DeepSpeed integration.\n\n## Purpose and Scope\n\nThe training system in OmniVoice is driven by centralized configuration files that define the model architecture, data sampling strategies, and optimization hyperparameters. The configuration is split into three main components:\n1.  **Training Config**: Global hyperparameters and system settings.\n2.  **Data Config**: Manifest paths and language-specific sampling weights.\n3.  **DeepSpeed Config**: Low-level optimization and memory management settings.\n\n## TrainingConfig Reference\n\nThe `TrainingConfig` class is the primary container for all training-related parameters. It is defined as a dataclass and is typically instantiated from a JSON file via the `from_json()` method [omnivoice/training/config.py:31-93]().\n\n### Model Architecture \u0026 Paths\n| Field | Type | Default | Description |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `llm_name_or_path` | `str` | `\"Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B\"` | Backbone LLM identifier or local path [omnivoice/training/config.py:37](). |\n| `audio_vocab_size` | `int` | `1025` | Total audio tokens including the mask token (valid vocab + 1) [omnivoice/training/config.py:38](). |\n| `audio_mask_id` | `int` | `1024` | The specific ID used for masking in the diffusion process [omnivoice/training/config.py:39](). |\n| `num_audio_codebook` | `int` | `8` | Number of hierarchical codebooks in the audio representation [omnivoice/training/config.py:40](). |\n| `init_from_checkpoint` | `str` | `None` | Path/ID to initialize weights for fine-tuning [omnivoice/training/config.py:56](). |\n| `resume_from_checkpoint`| `str` | `None` | Path to resume a paused training run (includes optimizer state) [omnivoice/training/config.py:55](). |\n\n### Training Hyperparameters\n| Field | Type | Default | Description |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `learning_rate` | `float` | `1e-4` | Peak learning rate [omnivoice/training/config.py:59](). |\n| `weight_decay` | `float` | `0.01` | L2 regularization factor [omnivoice/training/config.py:60](). |\n| `steps` | `int` | `300000` | Total number of training steps [omnivoice/training/config.py:62](). |\n| `lr_scheduler_type` | `str` | `\"cosine\"` | Type of learning rate scheduler [omnivoice/training/config.py:64](). |\n| `warmup_type` | `str` | `\"ratio\"` | Whether warmup is defined by `\"ratio\"` or `\"steps\"` [omnivoice/training/config.py:65](). |\n| `warmup_ratio` | `float` | `0.03` | Proportion of total steps for LR warmup [omnivoice/training/config.py:66](). |\n| `warmup_steps` | `int` | `2000` | Fixed number of warmup steps (if `warmup_type` is `\"steps\"`) [omnivoice/training/config.py:67-68](). |\n| `batch_tokens` | `int` | `8192` | Target number of tokens per batch (dynamic batching) [omnivoice/training/config.py:70](). |\n| `mixed_precision` | `str` | `\"bf16\"` | Precision mode (`fp16`, `bf16`, or `no`) [omnivoice/training/config.py:75](). |\n\n### Task-Specific Ratios\nThese parameters control the distribution of training tasks (Voice Cloning, Voice Design, etc.) and data augmentation during a single run:\n*   **`audio_codebook_weights`**: A list of weights applied to the CrossEntropy loss for each codebook layer, typically decreasing for deeper layers [omnivoice/training/config.py:43-45]().\n*   **`drop_cond_ratio` (0.1)**: Probability of dropping conditioning for Classifier-Free Guidance training [omnivoice/training/config.py:46]().\n*   **`prompt_ratio_range` (0.0, 0.3)**: Range for the length of the prompt prefix relative to the total sequence [omnivoice/training/config.py:47]().\n*   **`mask_ratio_range` (0.0, 1.0)**: Range for the proportion of tokens to be masked during training [omnivoice/training/config.py:48]().\n*   **`language_ratio` (0.8)**: Probability of including the language ID token [omnivoice/training/config.py:49]().\n*   **`use_pinyin_ratio` (0.3)**: Probability of using Pinyin representations for Chinese text [omnivoice/training/config.py:50]().\n*   **`instruct_ratio` (1.0)**: Probability of including instruction tokens for Voice Design [omnivoice/training/config.py:51]().\n*   **`only_instruct_ratio` (0.5)**: Ratio of instruction-only samples vs. mixed samples [omnivoice/training/config.py:52]().\n\n**Sources:**\n* [omnivoice/training/config.py:31-84]()\n\n## Data Configuration Format\n\nThe `data_config` JSON file specifies the location of training and development datasets. It allows for multi-dataset mixing and language-specific repetition.\n\n### Schema Structure\nThe JSON must contain two top-level keys: `\"train\"` and `\"dev\"`. Each is a list of dataset objects.\n\n```json\n{\n    \"train\": [\n        {\n            \"language_id\": \"en\",\n            \"manifest_path\": [\"path/to/en_train/data.lst\"],\n            \"repeat\": 1\n        },\n        {\n            \"language_id\": \"zh\",\n            \"manifest_path\": [\"path/to/zh_train/data.lst\"],\n            \"repeat\": 1\n        }\n    ],\n    \"dev\": [...]\n}\n```\n\n### Key Fields\n*   **`language_id`**: The ISO code or identifier for the language subset.\n*   **`manifest_path`**: A list of paths to `data.lst` files. These files contain pointers to the WebDataset `.tar` shards.\n*   **`repeat`**: Integer multiplier for oversampling specific datasets.\n\n**Sources:**\n* [omnivoice/training/config.py:34]()\n\n## DeepSpeed Configuration\n\nOmniVoice supports DeepSpeed for memory-efficient training, particularly useful for larger LLM backbones.\n\n### ZeRO Optimization\nThe default example uses ZeRO Stage 2, which partitions gradients and optimizer states across GPUs to reduce memory footprint [examples/config/ds_config_zero2.json:3-11]().\n\n### Auto-Configuration\nMany fields in the DeepSpeed config are set to `\"auto\"`. These values are dynamically populated by the `OmniTrainer` based on the `TrainingConfig` [examples/config/ds_config_zero2.json:12-18]().\n*   `train_batch_size`: Derived from `batch_tokens` and `gradient_accumulation_steps`.\n*   `bf16.enabled`: Derived from `mixed_precision`.\n*   `gradient_clipping`: Derived from `max_grad_norm`.\n\n**Sources:**\n* [examples/config/ds_config_zero2.json:1-20]()\n* [omnivoice/training/config.py:77-78]()\n\n## System Architecture Diagrams\n\n### Configuration Data Flow\nThis diagram illustrates how configuration files are ingested by the training entrypoint and distributed to internal components.\n\n**Configuration Ingestion Flow**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Filesystem\"\n        TC_JSON[\"train_config.json\"]\n        DC_JSON[\"data_config.json\"]\n        DS_JSON[\"ds_config.json\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"omnivoice.cli.train\"\n        CFG_LOAD[\"TrainingConfig.from_json()\"]\n        DATA_LOAD[\"Data Config Parser\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"omnivoice.training.trainer\"\n        TRAINER[\"OmniTrainer\"]\n        ACCEL[\"accelerate.Accelerator\"]\n    end\n\n    TC_JSON --\u003e CFG_LOAD\n    DC_JSON --\u003e DATA_LOAD\n    DS_JSON --\u003e ACCEL\n    \n    CFG_LOAD --\u003e TRAINER\n    DATA_LOAD --\u003e TRAINER\n    TRAINER --\u003e ACCEL\n```\n**Sources:**\n* [omnivoice/training/config.py:21-22]()\n* [omnivoice/training/config.py:87-93]()\n\n### Hyperparameter Mapping to Code Entities\nThis diagram maps `TrainingConfig` fields to the specific classes and logic they control within the codebase.\n\n**Parameter to Entity Mapping**\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"TrainingConfig Fields\"\n        BT[\"batch_tokens\"]\n        ACW[\"audio_codebook_weights\"]\n        LR[\"learning_rate\"]\n        DCR[\"drop_cond_ratio\"]\n        UPR[\"use_pinyin_ratio\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Code Entity Space\"\n        PDS[\"PackingIterableDataset\"]\n        COLL[\"PackingDataCollator\"]\n        MODEL[\"OmniVoiceModel\"]\n        OPT[\"torch.optim.AdamW\"]\n        PROC[\"OmniVoiceSampleProcessor\"]\n    end\n\n    BT -- \"Controls sequence packing\" --\u003e PDS\n    BT -- \"Sets batch dimension\" --\u003e COLL\n    ACW -- \"Weighted CrossEntropy loss\" --\u003e MODEL\n    LR -- \"Optimizer init\" --\u003e OPT\n    DCR -- \"Condition masking logic\" --\u003e PROC\n    UPR -- \"Text normalization/Pinyin injection\" --\u003e PROC\n```\n**Sources:**\n* [omnivoice/training/config.py:43-71]()\n* [examples/config/train_config_emilia.json:7-28]()\n\n## Example Configurations\n\nThe repository provides several templates in `examples/config/` for different scenarios:\n\n1.  **Emilia (Pre-training)**: Standard configuration for large-scale training on the Emilia dataset. It uses a learning rate of `1e-4` and focuses on the base audio modeling without heavy instruction tuning [examples/config/train_config_emilia.json:1-39]().\n2.  **Fine-tuning**: Reduced learning rate (`5e-5`) and fewer total steps (`5000`), initialized from the `k2-fsa/OmniVoice` checkpoint [examples/config/train_config_finetune.json:1-39]().\n3.  **Multilingual**: Higher `language_ratio` (0.8) and `instruct_ratio` (1.0) to emphasize cross-lingual capabilities and instruction-following (Voice Design) [examples/config/train_config_multilingual.json:1-39]().\n\n### Comparison of Ratios\n| Config | `language_ratio` | `instruct_ratio` | `use_pinyin_ratio` | `only_instruct_ratio` |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `train_config_emilia.json` | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |\n| `train_config_finetune.json` | 0.8 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |\n| `train_config_multilingual.json` | 0.8 | 1.0 | 0.3 | 0.5 |\n\n**Sources:**\n* [examples/config/train_config_emilia.json:8-15]()\n* [examples/config/train_config_finetune.json:8-15]()\n* [examples/config/train_config_multilingual.json:8-15]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"29:T1c01,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# Training Loop and Checkpointing\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [omnivoice/cli/train.py](omnivoice/cli/train.py)\n- [omnivoice/training/__init__.py](omnivoice/training/__init__.py)\n- [omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py](omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py)\n- [omnivoice/training/trainer.py](omnivoice/training/trainer.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThis page details the internals of the `OmniTrainer` class, the primary engine responsible for the OmniVoice training lifecycle. It covers the initialization of distributed environments via HuggingFace Accelerate and DeepSpeed, the configuration of optimizers and schedulers, the mechanics of the gradient accumulation loop, and the robust checkpointing system used for resuming long-running experiments.\n\n## System Architecture and Data Flow\n\nThe training process is orchestrated by `OmniTrainer`, which bridges the gap between the raw `OmniVoice` model and the distributed hardware. It manages the transition from data loading to loss computation and backpropagation.\n\n### Training Logic Flow\nThe following diagram illustrates how the CLI entrypoint initializes the system and enters the main training loop.\n\n**Diagram: Training Initialization and Loop Flow**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"CLI Entrypoint: omnivoice.cli.train\"\n        START[\"main()\"] --\u003e CFG[\"TrainingConfig.from_json()\"]\n        CFG --\u003e BUILD_MOD[\"build_model_and_tokenizer()\"]\n        CFG --\u003e BUILD_DATA[\"build_dataloaders()\"]\n        BUILD_MOD \u0026 BUILD_DATA --\u003e INIT_TRAINER[\"OmniTrainer.__init__()\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"OmniTrainer Internals\"\n        INIT_TRAINER --\u003e ACCEL[\"_init_accelerator()\"]\n        ACCEL --\u003e OPT[\"create_optimizer_and_scheduler()\"]\n        OPT --\u003e PREP[\"accelerator.prepare(model, optimizer, lr_scheduler)\"]\n        PREP --\u003e LOOP[\"train() Loop\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Main Loop Step\"\n        LOOP --\u003e FORWARD[\"model(**batch)\"]\n        FORWARD --\u003e LOSS[\"Compute Loss\"]\n        LOSS --\u003e BACK[\"accelerator.backward(loss)\"]\n        BACK --\u003e STEP[\"optimizer.step() \u0026 lr_scheduler.step()\"]\n        STEP --\u003e LOG[\"TrainLogger.log_metrics()\"]\n        LOG --\u003e SAVE[\"save_checkpoint() (Periodic)\"]\n        SAVE --\u003e LOOP\n    end\n```\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/cli/train.py:40-70](), [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:57-94](), [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:202-270]()\n\n---\n\n## Accelerator and DeepSpeed Initialization\n\nThe `OmniTrainer` uses `accelerate.Accelerator` to handle device placement, mixed precision (including TF32 support), and distributed strategies.\n\n*   **TF32 Support**: If `allow_tf32` is enabled in the config, the trainer sets `torch.set_float32_matmul_precision(\"high\")` [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:102-103]().\n*   **DeepSpeed Integration**: When `use_deepspeed` is active, the trainer initializes a `DeepSpeedPlugin` with the provided configuration path, handling gradient accumulation and clipping [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:110-121]().\n*   **DeepSpeed Batch Size Hack**: To accommodate the complex packing logic of the `PackingIterableDataset`, the trainer manually overrides `train_micro_batch_size_per_gpu` to 1 within the DeepSpeed configuration, as batching is handled upstream by the data loader [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:84-87]().\n*   **Logging**: The main process initializes `logging` to write to both `stdout` and a `train.log` file in the output directory [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:134-151]().\n\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:99-159]()\n\n---\n\n## Optimizer and Scheduler Setup\n\nThe trainer defaults to the `AdamW` optimizer and provides two primary learning rate scheduling strategies via the `create_optimizer_and_scheduler` method.\n\n| Component | Implementation | Configuration Source |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| **Optimizer** | `torch.optim.AdamW` | `learning_rate`, `weight_decay` |\n| **Warmup** | Linear | `warmup_steps` or `warmup_ratio` |\n| **Scheduler (Cosine)** | `get_cosine_schedule_with_warmup` | `lr_scheduler_type=\"cosine\"` |\n| **Scheduler (Constant)** | `get_constant_schedule_with_warmup` | `lr_scheduler_type=\"constant\"` |\n\nThe number of warmup steps can be defined either as a fixed integer or as a ratio of the total training steps [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:169-172]().\n\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:161-185]()\n\n---\n\n## Training Loop and Gradient Accumulation\n\nThe `train()` method executes the main loop. It supports resuming from a specific checkpoint using `load_checkpoint`, which restores the model, optimizer, and scheduler states [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:195-201]().\n\n### Step Logic\n1.  **Forward Pass**: Inputs are fetched from the `train_dataloader`.\n2.  **Gradient Accumulation**: The trainer uses `accelerator.accumulate(self.model)` context manager to handle multi-step gradients before performing an optimizer step [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:218-220]().\n3.  **Loss Scaling**: Handled automatically by the `Accelerator` during the backward pass [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:224]().\n4.  **Logging**: Every `logging_steps`, metrics (loss, learning rate) are sent to TensorBoard via the `TrainLogger` [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:236-247]().\n\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:202-270]()\n\n---\n\n## Checkpointing and Logging\n\nThe `TrainLogger` and checkpointing utilities ensure that training progress is visible and recoverable.\n\n### TrainLogger Class\nThe `TrainLogger` manages a `tqdm` progress bar and handles communication with Accelerate's trackers (e.g., TensorBoard).\n*   **Progress Bar**: Displays real-time loss and LR [omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py:72-83]().\n*   **Metric Formatting**: Automatically formats floating-point metrics for clean console output, using scientific notation for very small values [omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py:95-104]().\n\n### Checkpoint Management\nThe `save_checkpoint` function (aliased as `engine_save_checkpoint` in the trainer) performs a multi-stage save:\n1.  **Accelerator State**: Saves optimizer, scheduler, and RNG states using `accelerator.save_state()` [omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py:133]().\n2.  **Model State**: Saves the model in standard HuggingFace format (`config.json` + weights) using `save_pretrained` [omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py:136-141]().\n3.  **Tokenizer**: Saved alongside the model to ensure the checkpoint is self-contained [omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py:144-145]().\n4.  **Rotation**: Keeps only the last `N` checkpoints (default 3) to manage disk space [omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py:150-164]().\n\n**Diagram: Checkpoint Structure**\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Checkpoint Directory (checkpoint-STEP/)\"\n        A[\"pytorch_model.bin / .safetensors\"]\n        B[\"config.json\"]\n        C[\"tokenizer_config.json / vocab.json\"]\n        D[\"optimizer.bin\"]\n        E[\"scheduler.bin\"]\n        F[\"random_states.pkl\"]\n    end\n    \n    SAVE_FUNC[\"unwrap_model.save_pretrained()\"] --\u003e A \u0026 B\n    TOKEN_SAVE[\"tokenizer.save_pretrained()\"] --\u003e C\n    ACCEL_SAVE[\"accelerator.save_state()\"] --\u003e D \u0026 E \u0026 F\n```\n\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py:42-116](), [omnivoice/training/checkpoint.py:118-166](), [omnivoice/training/trainer.py:187-193]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"2a:T1c6b,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# Training Examples and Scripts\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [examples/README.md](examples/README.md)\n- [examples/config/data_config_finetune.json](examples/config/data_config_finetune.json)\n- [examples/run_emilia.sh](examples/run_emilia.sh)\n- [examples/run_finetune.sh](examples/run_finetune.sh)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThis page provides a practical guide to the shell scripts used for training and fine-tuning OmniVoice. These scripts automate the multi-stage process of data verification, audio tokenization, and model training using `accelerate`.\n\n## Overview of Training Scripts\n\nThe `examples/` directory contains pre-configured scripts for common training scenarios. These scripts serve as wrappers around the core CLI tools, managing environment variables, GPU allocation, and directory structures [examples/README.md:1-9]().\n\n| Script | Use Case | Primary Stages |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `run_emilia.sh` | Training from scratch | Data Check → Tokenization → Training |\n| `run_finetune.sh` | Fine-tuning | Tokenization → Training |\n| `run_eval.sh` | Evaluation | WER/CER → Similarity → UTMOS |\n\nSources: [examples/README.md:5-10]()\n\n---\n\n## The 3-Stage Pipeline: run_emilia.sh\n\nThe `run_emilia.sh` script is designed for large-scale training on the Emilia dataset. It implements a robust 3-stage pipeline to ensure data integrity before launching compute-intensive training [examples/run_emilia.sh:7-8]().\n\n### Stage 0: Data Verification\nThis stage checks for the physical presence of the raw Emilia dataset and the required JSONL manifests. It expects a specific directory structure under the `download/` directory [examples/run_emilia.sh:45-63]().\n\n- **Raw Data**: `download/Amphion___Emilia/raw/{EN,ZH}` [examples/run_emilia.sh:55-58]()\n- **Manifests**: `data/emilia/manifests/{split}.jsonl` where split is one of `emilia_en_dev`, `emilia_zh_dev`, `emilia_en_train`, or `emilia_zh_train` [examples/run_emilia.sh:72-74]().\n\n### Stage 1: Audio Tokenization\nRaw audio files are processed into discrete tokens using a neural audio codec. This stage uses `omnivoice.scripts.extract_audio_tokens` to generate WebDataset shards (`.tar` files) containing the 8-layer audio tokens [examples/run_emilia.sh:84-101](). It processes each split and outputs shards to `${TOKEN_DIR}/${split}/audios/` and metadata to `${TOKEN_DIR}/${split}/txts/` [examples/run_emilia.sh:93-94]().\n\n### Stage 2: Training Execution\nThe final stage invokes `accelerate launch` to start the `omnivoice.cli.train` module. It consumes the `TRAIN_CONFIG` and `data_config` JSON files to initialize the model and data loaders [examples/run_emilia.sh:105-115]().\n\n### Data Flow Diagram: run_emilia.sh\nThe following diagram illustrates how raw data transitions through the pipeline into a trained model checkpoint.\n\n**Emilia Training Pipeline Flow**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Stage 0: Verification\"\n        RAW[\"Raw Audio (.wav)\"]\n        JSONL[\"JSONL Manifests\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Stage 1: Tokenization\"\n        EXTRACT[\"omnivoice.scripts.extract_audio_tokens\"]\n        TOKENIZER[\"higgs-audio-v2-tokenizer\"]\n        SHARDS[\"WebDataset Shards (.tar)\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Stage 2: Training\"\n        TRAIN_CLI[\"omnivoice.cli.train\"]\n        ACCEL[\"accelerate launch\"]\n        CKPT[\"Model Checkpoints (.pt)\"]\n    end\n\n    RAW --\u003e EXTRACT\n    JSONL --\u003e EXTRACT\n    TOKENIZER -.-\u003e EXTRACT\n    EXTRACT --\u003e SHARDS\n    SHARDS --\u003e TRAIN_CLI\n    ACCEL --\u003e TRAIN_CLI\n    TRAIN_CLI --\u003e CKPT\n```\nSources: [examples/run_emilia.sh:45-115](), [examples/README.md:15-21]()\n\n---\n\n## Fine-tuning: run_finetune.sh\n\nThe `run_finetune.sh` script is optimized for adapting a pretrained OmniVoice model to custom datasets. It differs from the scratch training pipeline primarily in its configuration and initialization [examples/run_finetune.sh:1-3]().\n\n### Key Differences for Fine-tuning\nCompared to training from scratch, fine-tuning uses a lower learning rate and a smaller number of total steps [examples/README.md:91-96]().\n\n| Parameter | Fine-tuning Value | Source |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| `init_from_checkpoint` | `\"k2-fsa/OmniVoice\"` | [examples/README.md:93]() |\n| `learning_rate` | `5e-5` | [examples/README.md:95]() |\n| `steps` | `5,000` | [examples/README.md:94]() |\n\n### Input Data Format\nFine-tuning requires a JSONL manifest where each line represents a sample with mandatory fields [examples/README.md:58-63]():\n- `id`: Unique identifier.\n- `audio_path`: Absolute path to the source audio.\n- `text`: Transcription of the audio.\n- `language_id`: (Optional) ISO language code.\n\n### Script Logic: run_finetune.sh\nThe script iterates through `TRAIN_JSONL` and `DEV_JSONL` to generate tokens before launching the training process [examples/run_finetune.sh:42-83](). It uses the same `omnivoice.scripts.extract_audio_tokens` utility as the scratch training script [examples/run_finetune.sh:59-65]().\n\n**Fine-tuning Entity Mapping**\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Configuration Space\"\n        CONFIG[\"config/train_config_finetune.json\"]\n        DATA_CONFIG[\"config/data_config_finetune.json\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Code Execution\"\n        FINETUNE_SH[\"run_finetune.sh\"]\n        EXTRACT_MOD[\"omnivoice.scripts.extract_audio_tokens\"]\n        TRAIN_MOD[\"omnivoice.cli.train\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Model State\"\n        PRETRAINED[\"k2-fsa/OmniVoice\"]\n        NEW_CKPT[\"exp/omnivoice_finetune/\"]\n    end\n\n    FINETUNE_SH -- \"Stage 0\" --\u003e EXTRACT_MOD\n    FINETUNE_SH -- \"Stage 1\" --\u003e TRAIN_MOD\n    CONFIG -- \"init_from_checkpoint\" --\u003e PRETRAINED\n    PRETRAINED --\u003e TRAIN_MOD\n    TRAIN_MOD --\u003e NEW_CKPT\n    DATA_CONFIG --\u003e TRAIN_MOD\n```\nSources: [examples/run_finetune.sh:1-83](), [examples/README.md:52-98](), [examples/config/data_config_finetune.json:1-12]()\n\n---\n\n## Configuration and Control\n\nBoth scripts provide a set of variables at the top of the file to control the execution environment and stage range.\n\n### GPU and Directory Setup\nUsers should modify these variables to match their hardware and filesystem layout [examples/run_emilia.sh:11-39](), [examples/run_finetune.sh:11-35]():\n\n- `GPU_IDS`: Comma-separated list of GPU indices (e.g., `\"0,1\"`).\n- `NUM_GPUS`: Total number of GPUs for `accelerate`.\n- `TOKEN_DIR`: Where the intermediate `.tar` shards will be stored.\n- `OUTPUT_DIR`: Where the final model checkpoints and logs will be saved.\n- `TOKENIZER_PATH`: Path or HF repo for the audio tokenizer (default: `\"eustlb/higgs-audio-v2-tokenizer\"`) [examples/run_emilia.sh:30]().\n\n### Stage Range Control\nThe `stage` and `stop_stage` variables allow for resuming a pipeline or running specific parts in isolation [examples/run_emilia.sh:7-8]():\n\n- `stage=0, stop_stage=0`: Only verify data (or tokenize for fine-tuning).\n- `stage=1, stop_stage=1`: Only run tokenization (or training for fine-tuning).\n- `stage=2, stop_stage=2`: Only run training (Emilia specific).\n\n### Environment Variables\nThe scripts automatically set `PYTHONPATH` to the root of the repository to ensure that the `omnivoice` package and its internal modules are importable [examples/run_emilia.sh:42](), [examples/run_finetune.sh:38]().\n\nSources: [examples/run_emilia.sh:7-42](), [examples/run_finetune.sh:7-38]()"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"2b:T14e7,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"# Evaluation Framework\n\n\u003cdetails\u003e\n\u003csummary\u003eRelevant source files\u003c/summary\u003e\n\nThe following files were used as context for generating this wiki page:\n\n- [docs/evaluation.md](docs/evaluation.md)\n- [examples/run_eval.sh](examples/run_eval.sh)\n- [omnivoice/eval/__init__.py](omnivoice/eval/__init__.py)\n- [omnivoice/eval/utils.py](omnivoice/eval/utils.py)\n\n\u003c/details\u003e\n\n\n\nThe OmniVoice evaluation framework provides a comprehensive suite of tools to measure the performance of speech generation models across three primary dimensions: intelligibility, speaker similarity, and naturalness. The system supports multiple standardized test sets and leverages specialized Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) backends tailored to specific languages and datasets.\n\n## System Overview\n\nThe evaluation architecture is designed to be modular, allowing users to run individual metric scripts or execute a full pipeline across various benchmarks. It utilizes a centralized directory for evaluation models and datasets, typically managed via the `run_eval.sh` entry point.\n\n### Evaluation Workflow\n\nThe following diagram illustrates the relationship between the inference process and the evaluation subsystems:\n\n**OmniVoice Evaluation Pipeline**\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph \"Inference Space\"\n        A[\"omnivoice.cli.infer_batch\"] -- \"Generates\" --\u003e B[\"Audio Results (.wav)\"]\n        C[\"Test List (.jsonl)\"] -- \"Provides Metadata\" --\u003e A\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Metric Subsystems\"\n        B --\u003e D[\"WER/CER Subsystem\"]\n        B --\u003e E[\"Speaker Sim Subsystem\"]\n        B --\u003e F[\"UTMOS Subsystem\"]\n        \n        C --\u003e D\n        C --\u003e E\n        C --\u003e F\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Code Entity Space\"\n        D --- D1[\"omnivoice.eval.wer\"]\n        E --- E1[\"omnivoice.eval.speaker_similarity.sim\"]\n        F --- F1[\"omnivoice.eval.mos.utmos\"]\n    end\n```\n\n**Sources:** [docs/evaluation.md:1-13](), [examples/run_eval.sh:116-144]()\n\n## Supported Metrics\n\nOmniVoice evaluates generated speech using three industry-standard metrics:\n\n| Metric | Description | Key Code Entities |\n|---|---|---|\n| **WER / CER** | **Word/Character Error Rate**: Measures intelligibility by transcribing audio via ASR and comparing it to reference text. | `omnivoice.eval.wer` |\n| **SIM-o** | **Speaker Similarity**: Calculates the cosine similarity between embeddings of the reference audio and generated audio. | `omnivoice.eval.speaker_similarity.sim` |\n| **UTMOS** | **Naturalness (MOS)**: A neural network-based Mean Opinion Score (MOS) predictor that rates the \"human-likeness\" of audio. | `omnivoice.eval.mos.utmos` |\n\nFor details on the transcription backends and normalization, see [Word Error Rate (WER/CER) Evaluation](#6.1).\nFor details on the embedding models and similarity computation, see [Speaker Similarity (SIM-o) Evaluation](#6.2).\nFor details on the UTMOS architecture and scoring, see [UTMOS (Naturalness / MOS) Evaluation](#6.3).\n\n**Sources:** [docs/evaluation.md:34-48]()\n\n## Supported Test Sets\n\nThe framework includes automated scripts to download and evaluate on several major benchmarks. Each benchmark uses a specific ASR model to ensure consistency with existing literature.\n\n| Test Set | Language(s) | ASR Backend |\n|---|---|---|\n| **LibriSpeech-PC** | English | HuBERT-based ASR |\n| **Seed-TTS** | English, Chinese | Whisper (en) / Paraformer (zh) |\n| **FLEURS** | 102 Languages | Omnilingual-ASR |\n| **MiniMax** | 24 Languages | Whisper / Paraformer |\n\n**Sources:** [docs/evaluation.md:5-13](), [examples/run_eval.sh:52-61]()\n\n## Running the Evaluation Suite\n\nThe primary entry point for evaluation is the `examples/run_eval.sh` script. This script automates the multi-stage process of data preparation, batch inference, and metric calculation.\n\n### Execution Stages\n\n1.  **Stage 1: Preparation** - Downloads evaluation models from `k2-fsa/TTS_eval_models` and datasets from `k2-fsa/TTS_eval_datasets` [examples/run_eval.sh:67-109]().\n2.  **Stage 2-6: Benchmark Execution** - Performs batch inference using `omnivoice.cli.infer_batch` followed by metric computation for each specific test set [examples/run_eval.sh:116-215]().\n\n### Common Utilities\n\nThe evaluation system shares core audio processing logic in `omnivoice/eval/utils.py`. The `load_eval_waveform` function handles essential preprocessing tasks to ensure audio is in the correct format for evaluation models.\n\n**Evaluation Utility Mapping**\n```mermaid\ngraph LR\n    subgraph \"Utility Functions\"\n        LW[\"load_eval_waveform\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"Processing Logic\"\n        LW --\u003e SR[\"Resampling (torchaudio.functional.resample)\"]\n        LW --\u003e MC[\"Mono Conversion (mean)\"]\n        LW --\u003e TR[\"Truncation (max_seconds)\"]\n    end\n\n    subgraph \"File Location\"\n        LW --- F1[\"omnivoice/eval/utils.py\"]\n    end\n```\n\n**Sources:** [omnivoice/eval/utils.py:26-82](), [examples/run_eval.sh:15-43]()\n\n## Child Pages\n\n- [Word Error Rate (WER/CER) Evaluation](#6.1) — Details the ASR backends (HuBERT,
